title: The backlog that ate the broker
tagline: Your log-based queue's depth graph is a straight line going up. The pager is next.
brief: You run an event pipeline: an API tier appends events to a log-based message queue, a worker pool consumes them, results land in Postgres. Marketing just doubled traffic with a campaign. The producers are fine. The consumers say they're fine. The queue disagrees.
symptom: Watch the queue's depth counter in its node card and the memory% climbing in lock-step. Producers enqueue at 800 events/s; nothing errors — yet. Linear growth with no plateau means this never recovers on its own.

diagnosis.question: The queue's depth grows linearly for the whole run and never plateaus. What's the root cause?

options.text: Consumers drain slower than producers enqueue — total consumer throughput is below the 800 events/s arrival rate.
options.explain: Right. 1 consumer group × 5 consumers ÷ 10 ms = 500 events/s of drain against 800/s arriving. The 300/s difference accumulates forever — backlog is a rate mismatch, not a burst.

options.text: Postgres downstream is saturated and backpressuring the workers, which stalls consumption.
options.explain: Check Postgres's card: utilization is low and error rate is zero. The workers never receive more than the queue drains (500/s), well within DB capacity. The bottleneck is upstream of the workers.

options.text: maxDepth is too small — a bigger buffer would absorb the load.
options.explain: A bigger buffer buys minutes, not a fix. Arrival exceeds drain at steady state, so ANY finite buffer eventually fills. Buffers absorb bursts; they cannot fix a sustained rate mismatch.

options.text: Network latency between the producers and the queue is delaying enqueues.
options.explain: Wire latency delays individual events; it doesn't change throughput. The queue is RECEIVING the full 800/s just fine — that's exactly why its depth grows.

fix.objective: Make the pipeline drain at least as fast as it fills: keep the backlog flat at 800 events/s without crashing anything.
hints: Drain throughput = (consumer groups × consumers per group ÷ processing ms) × 1000. What does that come to right now, against 800/s arriving?
Click the Event Log node: more consumers per group, more groups, or faster per-event processing all raise drain. You need ≥ 800/s with headroom.
Doubling consumersPerGroup from 5 to 10 lifts drain to 1 000/s — enough, with 25% headroom.
